stem: A 4-year-old girl with confirmed trisomy 21 attends her annual general paediatric review at a metropolitan outpatient clinic. She had a complete repair of a complete AVSD at age 7 months and has no residual significant intracardiac shunt on her most recent echocardiogram 6 months ago. She is in mainstream preschool with aide support, has gained 1.4 kg over the past year along her usual centile on the Down syndrome growth chart, and her parents have no specific concerns. She is on no regular medications. Her most recent TSH 12 months ago was 4.2 mIU/L with a free T4 of 14 pmol/L, both within laboratory reference range. She has had no audiology concerns since her last test, no constipation, no cold intolerance, and her energy levels are normal for her. Her general examination today is unremarkable apart from typical syndromic facies, no goitre, and a flat well-healed midline sternotomy scar. Her mother asks specifically about routine bloods.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 96, RR 22, T 36.7, BP 95/60, SpO2 99% in air
data_table.Growth: Weight 14.8 kg (50th centile on T21 chart), Height 95 cm (25th centile on T21 chart)
data_table.Examination: Typical T21 features, no goitre, well-healed sternotomy, no peripheral oedema
data_table.Previous investigations: TSH 4.2 mIU/L, fT4 14 pmol/L (12 months ago)

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate thyroid screening plan for this child?

options.text: Repeat TSH and free T4 only if symptoms of hypothyroidism develop
options.rationale: Symptom-based screening misses subclinical hypothyroidism, which is common in trisomy 21 and frequently asymptomatic; routine biochemical surveillance is recommended regardless of symptoms (RCH CPG: Down syndrome - health surveillance).

options.text: Repeat TSH and free T4 every 6 months until age 5, then annually
options.rationale: Six-monthly testing is recommended only in the first year of life when congenital hypothyroidism risk is highest; from 12 months onwards annual screening is sufficient unless biochemistry is abnormal (RCH CPG: Down syndrome).

options.text: Annual TSH (with free T4 if TSH abnormal) lifelong
options.rationale: Australian and international Down syndrome guidance recommends annual thyroid screening from age 1 onwards, lifelong, because of the high cumulative incidence of acquired hypothyroidism and frequent subclinical disease in this population (RCH CPG: Down syndrome - health surveillance; AAP clinical report: Down syndrome).

options.text: TSH, free T4 and thyroid autoantibodies every 3 years
options.rationale: Three-yearly testing is too infrequent given high background incidence of hypothyroidism in T21; routine thyroid autoantibody testing is not standard and does not change the annual screening interval (RCH CPG: Down syndrome).

options.text: Refer to paediatric endocrinology for routine surveillance
options.rationale: Specialist endocrinology referral is reserved for abnormal results or clinical concerns; uncomplicated annual screening can be performed in primary or general paediatric care (RCH CPG: Down syndrome).

explanation.summary: Children with trisomy 21 have a markedly increased lifetime risk of both congenital and acquired hypothyroidism, much of it subclinical. Routine annual TSH surveillance from age 1 onwards is recommended in Australian and international guidance (RCH CPG: Down syndrome; AAP Down syndrome health supervision).
key_points: Congenital hypothyroidism is screened on the newborn screen; thereafter, T21 children need ongoing periodic testing
Standard schedule: at birth (newborn screen), at 6 and 12 months, then annually for life
Annual surveillance even in asymptomatic, biochemically normal patients (RCH CPG: Down syndrome)
explanation.pearls: Symptoms of hypothyroidism are easily missed in T21 because hypotonia, constipation, and dry skin overlap with the syndromic baseline - biochemistry, not symptoms, drives detection.

subtopic_detail: Down syndrome - atlantoaxial instability and sport clearance
stem: A 9-year-old boy with trisomy 21 attends his paediatrician for clearance to join a local junior trampolining club, having previously only participated in school swimming. His parents are keen because of his social benefit but want a clear answer on safety. He had a balanced AVSD repair at 5 months with no residual lesion and has been discharged from cardiology. He attends a mainstream school with aide support and his TSH and full blood count from 4 months ago were normal. He has had no neck pain, no torticollis, no changes in gait, no recent decline in fine motor skills, no urinary incontinence, and no clumsiness compared with his usual baseline. His neurological examination today shows the expected mild generalised hypotonia of trisomy 21, symmetric reflexes with no clonus or upgoing plantars, normal tone in all four limbs, and a gait that is age-typical for him with no ataxia. He has never had a cervical spine X-ray. His mother asks whether he requires imaging or specialist review before starting trampolining, swimming and football at his new school.

data_table.Examination: Typical T21 features, mild generalised hypotonia, no torticollis, symmetric reflexes, plantars downgoing, no clonus, age-appropriate gait

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step before he begins trampolining?

options.text: Order routine flexion-extension and lateral cervical spine radiographs before clearing him for any sport participation
options.rationale: Routine screening cervical spine radiographs in asymptomatic children with Down syndrome are no longer recommended because they have poor predictive value for clinically important atlantoaxial instability and do not change management in asymptomatic children (RCH CPG: Down syndrome; AAP Down syndrome health supervision).

options.text: Clinical screen with focused history and neurological examination, and counsel that trampolining is high-risk and best avoided
options.rationale: Current guidance is symptom and examination based: ask about neck pain, torticollis, gait change, weakness, sphincter changes; examine for myelopathic signs. Sports involving forced cervical flexion or risk of head-first impact (trampolining, gymnastics, diving, rugby contact, boxing) are specifically discouraged in T21 because of the elevated background risk of atlantoaxial instability (RCH CPG: Down syndrome; AAP Down syndrome health supervision).

options.text: Refer urgently for MRI of the cervical spine before clearing him for any sport participation
options.rationale: MRI is reserved for children with symptoms or signs suggestive of cervical cord compression (neck pain, torticollis, gait deterioration, new incontinence, hyperreflexia); it is not routinely indicated in asymptomatic children for sports screening (RCH CPG: Down syndrome).

options.text: Clear him for trampolining and all contact sports given the entirely normal neurological examination today
options.rationale: A normal examination does not exclude atlantoaxial instability and does not negate the elevated risk inherent to T21; trampolining, gymnastics, diving and contact rugby remain discouraged regardless of a normal examination (AAP Down syndrome health supervision).

options.text: Restrict him from all organised competitive sport until a paediatric orthopaedic spine specialist review
options.rationale: Blanket sport restriction is not recommended and would deprive him of significant developmental and social benefit; swimming, football of certain codes and athletics are encouraged with appropriate selection (RCH CPG: Down syndrome).

explanation.summary: Routine cervical spine radiography for asymptomatic children with Down syndrome has been abandoned in current Australian and AAP guidance because of poor predictive value. Sports clearance relies on a focused symptom-and-examination screen plus counselling about avoiding activities with forced cervical flexion or head-first impact, such as trampolining and gymnastics (RCH CPG: Down syndrome).
key_points: Screen by history (neck pain, torticollis, gait change, new incontinence, weakness) and examination (tone, reflexes, clonus, plantars, gait)
Trampolining, gymnastics, diving, boxing and certain contact codes are discouraged regardless of imaging
Image only when symptoms or signs raise concern, then with MRI for cord assessment
explanation.pearls: Atlantoaxial instability in T21 presents with subtle changes: torticollis, decline in handwriting or gait, new toe-walking, or new incontinence - all warrant urgent neurosurgical and imaging review.

stem: A 3-year-old boy with trisomy 21 is brought to the GP by his mother because of 3 weeks of progressive lethargy, intermittent low-grade fevers without an obvious source, and new bruising over his shins and trunk that she does not recall him sustaining. He had complete AVSD repair at 6 months with no current cardiac issues and a normal TSH 5 months ago. He has otherwise been well, fully immunised, on no medications, and has no preceding viral illness or anti-inflammatory exposure. On examination he is pale, has a smooth firm spleen palpable 3 cm below the left costal margin, generalised non-tender cervical lymphadenopathy with the largest node 2 cm in diameter, and multiple bruises in non-traumatic distribution including a few petechiae on the soft palate. There is no rash, no joint swelling, and no focus of infection. He is haemodynamically stable. A point-of-care capillary blood count is sent urgently from the practice.

data_table.Examination: Pale, splenomegaly 3 cm BLCM, generalised lymphadenopathy (largest 2 cm), bruising on shins and trunk, palatal petechiae
data_table.Investigations: Hb 68 g/L, WCC 22 x10^9/L (blasts on film), platelets 28 x10^9/L

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Reassure as likely viral illness, repeat full blood count in 2 weeks
options.rationale: Pancytopenia, blasts on film, splenomegaly and lymphadenopathy in any child mandate urgent specialist assessment; repeat in 2 weeks risks deterioration and delay of leukaemia diagnosis (RCH CPG: Approach to suspected leukaemia).

options.text: Prescribe oral antibiotics for presumed occult bacterial infection and review in 48 hours
options.rationale: Empirical oral antibiotics without a defined infection focus delay diagnosis and do not address the underlying haematological abnormality; the picture is bone marrow failure with circulating blasts, not undifferentiated infection (RCH CPG: Suspected leukaemia).

options.text: Arrange immediate transfer to a tertiary paediatric oncology service for marrow assessment
options.rationale: Pancytopenia with circulating blasts, hepatosplenomegaly and lymphadenopathy in a child with trisomy 21 is leukaemia until proven otherwise. Children with T21 carry approximately a 10 to 20-fold increased lifetime risk of acute leukaemia (both ALL and AMKL), and urgent same-day transfer for bone marrow biopsy and supportive care is required (RCH CPG: Suspected leukaemia; AAP Down syndrome health supervision).

options.text: Order ferritin, B12 and folate to investigate the anaemia in primary care
options.rationale: Nutritional anaemia work-up does not explain the thrombocytopenia, blasts, splenomegaly, or lymphadenopathy and would delay a leukaemia diagnosis requiring tertiary care (RCH CPG: Suspected leukaemia).

options.text: Refer to outpatient general paediatrics for next available clinic appointment
options.rationale: An outpatient appointment delays definitive diagnosis and supportive care; this child needs same-day tertiary oncology assessment given marrow failure and circulating blasts (RCH CPG: Suspected leukaemia).

explanation.summary: Trisomy 21 confers a substantially increased risk of acute leukaemia: ALL across childhood and a unique predisposition to acute megakaryoblastic leukaemia (AMKL) under age 4. Pancytopenia with circulating blasts demands urgent tertiary referral for marrow examination (RCH CPG: Suspected leukaemia).
key_points: Approximately 10-20-fold increased risk of leukaemia in trisomy 21 compared with the general paediatric population
AMKL predominates under age 4, ALL throughout childhood
Pancytopenia + blasts + organomegaly = same-day tertiary referral
explanation.pearls: Transient abnormal myelopoiesis in T21 neonates resolves spontaneously in most cases but predisposes to AMKL in the first 4 years - prior TAM is a flag for vigilance.

stem: A 6-year-old girl with karyotypically confirmed 45,X Turner syndrome is reviewed in a tertiary endocrinology and general paediatric clinic. She was diagnosed in the newborn period after she presented with lymphoedema and a webbed neck; echocardiogram in the neonatal period showed a normally functioning bicuspid aortic valve with no coarctation. She is on subcutaneous growth hormone and her height has tracked along the Turner syndrome centile chart since starting therapy 18 months ago. Her parents ask about the schedule of ongoing heart investigations because they have read about aortic complications in adult women with Turner syndrome. She is asymptomatic, has no exertional symptoms, plays normally with peers, and has no chest pain, syncope, or palpitations. On examination she has the expected Turner phenotype, a normal cardiac auscultation with no murmur today, and equal brachial and femoral pulses with no inter-limb blood pressure differential. Her last echocardiogram was 18 months ago and was unchanged.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 92, RR 20, BP right arm 102/65, right leg 104/68, no inter-limb gradient
data_table.Examination: Turner phenotype, no murmur, S1 S2 normal, femoral pulses equal to brachial
data_table.Imaging: Bicuspid aortic valve known, last echo 18 months ago - no aortic dilatation

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate ongoing cardiac surveillance plan?

options.text: Repeat the echocardiogram only if a new cardiac murmur or new symptom develops in future
options.rationale: Symptom-based surveillance misses progressive aortic dilatation, which is frequently silent in Turner syndrome and is a major cause of premature mortality in adulthood (Australasian Turner Syndrome Clinical Care Guidelines; International Turner Syndrome Guideline 2017).

options.text: Echocardiogram every 5 years through childhood, then transition imaging to the adult cardiology service
options.rationale: Five-yearly imaging is insufficient given the lifetime risk of aortic root dilatation and dissection; current guidelines recommend more frequent imaging through childhood with transition imaging at adolescence (International Turner Syndrome Guideline 2017).

options.text: Annual blood pressure measurement and ECG without further imaging unless symptoms arise
options.rationale: Blood pressure and ECG do not exclude aortic root dilatation or evolving coarctation, both of which require imaging (echocardiogram and ultimately MRI) for surveillance (International Turner Syndrome Guideline 2017).

options.text: Serial childhood echocardiography (typically every 3 to 5 years) with a baseline cardiac MRI at adolescent transition
options.rationale: Current guidance recommends serial childhood echocardiography (typically every 3 to 5 years if the heart and aorta are normal, sooner if abnormal), with a transition cardiac MRI in adolescence to establish a baseline of the aortic dimensions for ongoing adult surveillance; this woman will need lifelong imaging because of the aortopathy risk associated with 45,X and bicuspid aortic valve (International Turner Syndrome Guideline 2017; Australian paediatric endocrine practice).

options.text: Cardiac MRI annually from age 6 onwards regardless of cardiac symptoms or echo findings
options.rationale: Annual MRI in a young child generally requires sedation and is not warranted in an asymptomatic patient with normal recent echocardiography; serial echocardiography with a planned transition MRI is the standard approach (International Turner Syndrome Guideline 2017).

explanation.summary: Women with Turner syndrome have a substantially elevated lifetime risk of bicuspid aortic valve, coarctation, aortic root dilatation, and dissection. Australian and international guidance recommends serial echocardiography through childhood, with a baseline cardiac MRI at adolescent transition and lifelong adult surveillance (International Turner Syndrome Guideline 2017).
key_points: Bicuspid aortic valve in 15-30%, coarctation in 7-12% of Turner syndrome cases
Aortic dissection risk is markedly elevated, particularly with bicuspid valve, coarctation, hypertension or pregnancy
Childhood echocardiography with transition MRI sets the baseline for adult surveillance
explanation.pearls: Indexed aortic size to body surface area (z-score) is used in children; in adults, ascending aortic size index >2.5 cm/m^2 flags very high dissection risk.

stem: A 16-month-old girl with genetically confirmed Williams syndrome (7q11.23 microdeletion on chromosomal microarray) is reviewed in a tertiary metabolic clinic after a routine annual calcium check showed an elevated corrected calcium. She has known supravalvular aortic stenosis with a peak gradient of 22 mmHg under cardiology follow-up, the characteristic elfin facies, and a sociable, indiscriminately friendly behavioural profile. Over the past 6 weeks her parents describe increased irritability, constipation despite adequate dietary fibre, polyuria with new night-time wetting after months of dry nappies, and reduced appetite. She has been receiving a standard infant multivitamin including 400 IU/day vitamin D and her formula has been a regular age-appropriate cows-milk-based formula. She has had no recent vitamin D loading, no thiazide diuretic exposure, no calcium supplements, and no excessive sun exposure unusual for Brisbane. She is otherwise well, has no fever, no rash, and no signs of dehydration on examination today. The team plans an outpatient management approach but is reviewing dietary, supplement and pharmacological options.

data_table.Examination: Elfin facies, no goitre, no peripheral oedema, abdomen soft, no palpable mass, no signs of dehydration
data_table.Investigations: Corrected calcium 3.10 mmol/L, ionised calcium 1.55 mmol/L, PTH suppressed 0.4 pmol/L, 25-OH vitamin D 78 nmol/L, phosphate 1.2 mmol/L, creatinine 28 micromol/L, urinary calcium:creatinine ratio elevated
data_table.Imaging: Renal ultrasound: mild bilateral medullary nephrocalcinosis
data_table.Medications: Multivitamin with 400 IU vitamin D daily

lead_in: Which single intervention is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Increase her oral fluid intake only and recheck the serum calcium in 6 weeks without other change
options.rationale: Hydration alone, without removing the offending vitamin D and limiting dietary calcium, will not address the underlying problem and risks progressive nephrocalcinosis (AAP Williams syndrome health supervision).

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day and continue her current diet and vitamin D supplements unchanged
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are reserved for hypercalcaemia refractory to dietary and supplement adjustment; first-line management is removal of vitamin D and calcium restriction with specialist input (AAP Williams syndrome health supervision).

options.text: Commence a single intravenous infusion of pamidronate in the day-stay unit and continue current diet
options.rationale: Bisphosphonates are reserved for severe, symptomatic, or refractory hypercalcaemia and have long-lasting effects on bone in young children; they are not first-line in mild-to-moderate Williams syndrome-related hypercalcaemia (AAP Williams syndrome health supervision; RCH CPG: Hypercalcaemia).

options.text: Admit for intravenous fluid rehydration and intravenous frusemide infusion to promote urinary calcium excretion
options.rationale: Loop diuretics promote calciuria but are reserved for severe symptomatic hypercalcaemia with adequate hydration support; in mild outpatient-manageable hypercalcaemia, removing the exogenous calcium and vitamin D source is sufficient and avoids hypokalaemia and dehydration (RCH CPG: Hypercalcaemia).

options.text: Stop the vitamin D-containing multivitamin and start a low-calcium, vitamin D-free formula with endocrine input
options.rationale: Idiopathic infantile hypercalcaemia is a recognised feature of Williams syndrome and the first-line intervention is to remove exogenous vitamin D, switch to a low-calcium specialty formula, and limit dietary calcium under specialist endocrine and dietetic guidance; this approach typically normalises calcium without bisphosphonate or steroid escalation (Williams Syndrome Association clinical care recommendations; AAP Health care supervision for children with Williams syndrome).

explanation.summary: Children with Williams syndrome are predisposed to idiopathic infantile hypercalcaemia. The first-line approach for mild-to-moderate hypercalcaemia is dietary calcium restriction and vitamin D withdrawal with paediatric endocrine guidance; pharmacological escalation (steroids, bisphosphonates, loop diuretics) is reserved for refractory or severe cases (AAP Williams syndrome health supervision).
key_points: Annual calcium and urinary calcium:creatinine screening is recommended in Williams syndrome
Renal ultrasound assesses for nephrocalcinosis when hypercalcaemia is detected
Use low-calcium specialty formula and avoid vitamin D supplementation as first-line management
explanation.pearls: Suppressed PTH plus hypercalcaemia + nephrocalcinosis + elfin facies + supravalvular AS is a syndrome-defining cluster - send microarray for 7q11.23 deletion.

subtopic_detail: Noonan syndrome - feeding and cardiac surveillance
stem: An 8-month-old boy referred from a regional paediatrician now has a confirmed PTPN11 pathogenic variant consistent with Noonan syndrome. His parents are seen in a metropolitan multidisciplinary Noonan clinic. He has known mild-to-moderate valvular pulmonary stenosis on his most recent echocardiogram with a peak gradient of 35 mmHg and no clinical decompensation. He has been a slow and reluctant feeder since birth, taking 60 to 90 minutes for a bottle, with frequent posseting, occasional choking on thin fluids, and a weight tracking along the 3rd centile despite caloric fortification advice. He has had no aspiration pneumonias to date. He has the typical Noonan facies with hypertelorism, downslanting palpebral fissures, low-set posteriorly rotated ears, a webbed neck, and a pectus carinatum-excavatum combination. Developmentally he is mildly delayed in gross motor but has age-appropriate social engagement. Bleeding history is unremarkable with no easy bruising and no prolonged bleeding from heel pricks. His parents ask which specialist input is most important to add now to address the issue limiting his weight gain.

data_table.Growth: Weight 6.4 kg (3rd centile), Length 65 cm (10th centile)
data_table.Examination: Noonan facies, 3/6 ESM at upper LSE radiating to back, normal femoral pulses, no hepatomegaly
data_table.Investigations: Echo: valvular PS, peak gradient 35 mmHg, normal RV function; FBC normal; coagulation screen pending

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next addition to his multidisciplinary management?

options.text: Refer to a paediatric feeding service for multidisciplinary assessment with speech pathology and dietetics
options.rationale: Feeding difficulties are extremely common in Noonan syndrome and a leading cause of failure to thrive in infancy. A coordinated feeding assessment (speech pathology for swallow safety, dietetics for caloric optimisation, occupational therapy for sensory feeding behaviours) addresses the dominant clinical problem in this child (Noonan Syndrome Clinical Management Guidelines; RCH CPG: Failure to thrive).
source_refs: Noonan syndrome clinical management guidelines (van der Burgt)

options.text: Refer urgently to cardiology for balloon pulmonary valvuloplasty to relieve the pulmonary stenosis gradient
options.rationale: Intervention on the pulmonary valve in Noonan syndrome is typically reserved for severe stenosis (peak gradient >60 mmHg) or symptomatic disease, with reduced efficacy of balloon valvuloplasty given the dysplastic valve morphology that often requires surgery; a gradient of 35 mmHg with no decompensation does not meet that threshold (Noonan syndrome clinical management guidelines).
source_refs: Noonan syndrome clinical management guidelines (van der Burgt)

options.text: Commence subcutaneous growth hormone therapy now to address his persistent short stature and weight
options.rationale: Growth hormone in Noonan syndrome is considered later in childhood after expert endocrinology assessment and is not appropriate as the next step for an 8-month-old with active feeding difficulties; addressing nutritional intake is the priority (Noonan syndrome clinical management guidelines).
source_refs: Noonan syndrome clinical management guidelines (van der Burgt)

options.text: Refer to paediatric haematology now for prophylactic platelet transfusions and ongoing infusion planning
options.rationale: Bleeding diathesis is common in Noonan syndrome and requires baseline coagulation and platelet function assessment, but routine prophylactic platelet transfusions are not indicated in an asymptomatic infant with no bleeding history (Noonan syndrome clinical management guidelines).
source_refs: Noonan syndrome clinical management guidelines (van der Burgt)

options.text: Refer the parents to clinical genetics for counselling alone, without further specialist clinical input
options.rationale: Genetic counselling is part of comprehensive Noonan care, but his immediate growth and feeding trajectory requires active intervention; counselling alone does not address the failure to thrive (Noonan syndrome clinical management guidelines).
source_refs: Noonan syndrome clinical management guidelines (van der Burgt)

explanation.summary: Noonan syndrome management is multidisciplinary, with feeding difficulties and growth failure dominating infancy in a substantial minority of children. Coordinated speech-pathology-led feeding assessment with dietetic input is the immediate priority once cardiac disease is stable below intervention thresholds (Noonan syndrome clinical management guidelines).
key_points: Pulmonary stenosis is the most common cardiac lesion (around 50%) and is often dysplastic, frequently requiring surgical rather than balloon intervention if severe
Bleeding diathesis is common - check FBC, coagulation, and consider von Willebrand and platelet function before any surgery
Growth hormone consideration is later in childhood, after feeding and growth optimisation
explanation.pearls: Noonan syndrome is autosomal dominant - examine parents for subtle phenotype and consider parental molecular testing once the proband variant is known.

sources.label: Noonan syndrome clinical management guidelines (van der Burgt)

stem: A 12-year-old girl is referred by her GP to a paediatric cardiology and genetics service because her father was recently diagnosed with Marfan syndrome at age 41 after an acute type A aortic dissection that he survived following emergency surgery. A pathogenic FBN1 variant was identified on his diagnostic genetic panel. She is asymptomatic, plays netball at school, and has no chest pain, palpitations, syncope, or visual symptoms. She has never had an ophthalmology slit-lamp examination or an echocardiogram. On clinical examination today she is tall for her age and family (height 168 cm, 95th centile; mid-parental centile 50th), with a moderate pectus excavatum, mild thoracolumbar scoliosis on standing radiograph (Cobb 18 degrees), a positive Steinberg sign on the right, and an arm span to height ratio of 1.04. She has no spontaneous pneumothorax history, no skin striae beyond unremarkable post-pubertal striae, and no dural ectasia symptoms.

data_table.Examination: Tall, pectus excavatum, mild scoliosis (Cobb 18), positive right Steinberg, arm span:height 1.04, no Walker-Murdoch, no high-arched palate, no skin hyperextensibility
data_table.Family history: Father - pathogenic FBN1 variant, type A dissection at 41 (survived)

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Order whole-exome sequencing now as the first-line genetic investigation for this asymptomatic teenager
options.rationale: Whole-exome sequencing is not appropriate as first-line when a specific familial variant is already known; cascade testing of the single variant is more efficient and definitive (Marfan Foundation clinical care recommendations).

options.text: Order a full multi-gene aortopathy panel (FBN1, TGFBR1/2, ACTA2, MYH11) as first-line genetic testing
options.rationale: Multi-gene panels are first-line when no familial variant is known; when a specific familial pathogenic variant is identified, cascade testing of that variant is faster, cheaper, and definitive (Marfan Foundation clinical care recommendations).

options.text: Reassure that her habitus is familial and rescreen only if she later develops cardiovascular symptoms
options.rationale: Symptom-based screening in a first-degree relative of a confirmed pathogenic FBN1 variant carrier is unsafe; ascending aortic dilatation is usually asymptomatic until dissection and her phenotype (tall stature, pectus excavatum, scoliosis, positive Steinberg) already raises the index of suspicion (Revised Ghent criteria 2010).

options.text: Refer for empirical prophylactic beta-blocker therapy without genetic testing or baseline aortic imaging today
options.rationale: Initiation of medical therapy is appropriate after a diagnosis is made and aortic dimensions documented; commencing beta-blockade before confirming Marfan syndrome or measuring the baseline aortic root is not evidence-based (Marfan Foundation clinical care recommendations).

options.text: Targeted cascade FBN1 testing for the familial variant, plus baseline echocardiogram and ophthalmology slit-lamp assessment
options.rationale: When a familial pathogenic FBN1 variant is known, targeted (cascade) genetic testing for that single variant is the most efficient confirmatory test in at-risk first-degree relatives, alongside baseline imaging of the aorta (echocardiogram +/- subsequent MRI/CT) and an ophthalmology assessment for ectopia lentis; the revised Ghent criteria include both genetic and clinical pathways to diagnosis (Revised Ghent criteria 2010; Marfan Foundation clinical care guidelines).

explanation.summary: When a familial pathogenic FBN1 variant is known, first-degree relatives undergo cascade targeted genetic testing combined with baseline aortic imaging and ophthalmology assessment. The revised Ghent criteria explicitly incorporate genetic findings to confirm or exclude Marfan syndrome in relatives (Revised Ghent criteria 2010).
key_points: Cascade testing of a known familial variant is the most efficient genetic strategy
Echocardiogram measures aortic root Z-score and screens for mitral valve prolapse
Ophthalmology slit-lamp examination is essential to detect ectopia lentis
explanation.pearls: A negative cascade test for a known familial pathogenic FBN1 variant effectively excludes Marfan syndrome in that relative and ends ongoing surveillance.

subtopic_detail: Marfan syndrome - aortic protection and activity
stem: A 15-year-old boy with previously diagnosed Marfan syndrome on the basis of revised Ghent criteria and a confirmed FBN1 pathogenic variant attends his annual paediatric cardiology review at a tertiary centre. He plays competitive rowing five times per week, including ergometer sessions involving repetitive Valsalva. His most recent transthoracic echocardiogram shows an aortic root at the sinuses of Valsalva of 4.0 cm with a Z-score of 3.2 indexed to body surface area, increased from a Z-score of 2.5 one year ago. There is mild aortic regurgitation. He has no history of dissection, no syncope, no chest pain, and no documented hypertension. He is currently not on any cardiovascular medications, having declined them previously because his aortic root was within range. He has no asthma, no diabetes, and no contraindications to beta-blockade or angiotensin receptor blockade. His mother died of a presumed type A dissection in her late 30s. He is keen to continue elite rowing if at all possible and is also asking whether he should continue resistance training for rugby.

data_table.Examination: Tall Marfanoid habitus, soft early diastolic murmur at LSE consistent with mild AR, no signs of heart failure
data_table.Imaging: Aortic root 4.0 cm, Z-score 3.2 (was 2.5 one year ago), mild aortic regurgitation, no dissection

lead_in: Which single management plan is most appropriate?

options.text: Continue the current competitive rowing programme, defer medication, and review again in 12 months' time
options.rationale: An aortic root Z-score that has increased from 2.5 to 3.2 over 12 months warrants both pharmacological aortic protection and modification of high-intensity isometric and burst-Valsalva sports (Marfan Foundation clinical care recommendations; AHA / Bethesda Conference guidance on athletic participation in Marfan syndrome).

options.text: Commence beta-blocker (or losartan if intolerant), restrict competitive isometric and contact sport, repeat imaging every 6 months
options.rationale: An aortic root Z-score of 3.2 with rapid progression warrants commencement of beta-blockade (or angiotensin receptor blocker such as losartan if intolerant), avoidance of competitive contact, isometric and burst-Valsalva sports, and intensified imaging surveillance every 6 months. Moderate aerobic exercise is encouraged (Marfan Foundation clinical care recommendations; AHA / Bethesda Conference guidance).

options.text: Refer immediately for prophylactic aortic root replacement surgery despite the current aortic root diameter measurement
options.rationale: Elective prophylactic aortic root replacement is generally offered at aortic root diameter approximately 5.0 cm in adults with Marfan syndrome (or smaller with rapid progression, family history of dissection, or planned pregnancy); 4.0 cm with mild AR does not meet threshold (Marfan Foundation clinical care recommendations).

options.text: Commence a beta-blocker but allow unrestricted rowing and rugby resistance work because medication is fully protective
options.rationale: Pharmacotherapy does not substitute for activity modification; competitive contact, isometric and burst-Valsalva sport are restricted in Marfan syndrome irrespective of medication (Bethesda / AHA exercise guidance).

options.text: Stop all exercise indefinitely and avoid any organised or recreational physical activity going forward
options.rationale: Complete activity cessation is not recommended; moderate aerobic, non-isometric, non-competitive exercise is actively encouraged because of overall cardiovascular and psychosocial benefit (Bethesda / AHA exercise guidance Marfan).

explanation.summary: Aortic protection in Marfan syndrome combines pharmacotherapy (beta-blockade or angiotensin receptor blockade), activity modification (avoid competitive contact, isometric and burst-Valsalva sport, continue moderate aerobic activity), and serial imaging. Surgical thresholds are 5.0 cm aortic root, or smaller with rapid progression, family history of early dissection, planned pregnancy, or significant aortic regurgitation (Marfan Foundation clinical care recommendations).
key_points: Beta-blockade and angiotensin receptor blockade slow aortic root growth in Marfan syndrome
Family history of early dissection lowers the surgical threshold
Restrict competitive isometric/contact sports; encourage moderate aerobic exercise
explanation.pearls: Indexed aortic Z-score and rate of change matter more than a single absolute diameter when deciding to start or escalate therapy in a growing adolescent.

stem: A 17-year-old woman is referred to a paediatric genetics service from a regional clinic for assessment of suspected Ehlers-Danlos syndrome. Her sister, aged 21, was recently admitted to a metropolitan tertiary hospital after a spontaneous splenic artery rupture and underwent emergency embolisation; sister's subsequent connective tissue gene panel confirmed a pathogenic COL3A1 variant. The patient herself has generalised joint hypermobility with a Beighton score of 7/9, soft velvety skin, easy bruising in unusual locations (forearms, thighs) that her mother has long attributed to clumsiness, and translucent skin over the chest wall with prominent visible veins. She has had no major vascular events, but reports two episodes of mild epistaxis in the past year and a self-resolving spontaneous left-sided pneumothorax aged 14 (managed conservatively). She has thin lips, a thin nose, and prominent eyes. She has no marfanoid habitus, no aortic root dilatation on a recent echocardiogram (root 2.6 cm, Z-score 0.4), no ectopia lentis, no scoliosis, and a normal palate. Her current GP queried hypermobile EDS at age 14 based on the Beighton score and skin findings. She is asking about her diagnosis, her own pregnancy risk in future, and what surveillance she needs.

data_table.Examination: Beighton 7/9, soft velvety translucent skin, visible chest wall veins, easy bruising, thin lips, thin nose, prominent eyes; no marfanoid habitus, no aortic root dilatation
data_table.Family history: Sister with confirmed COL3A1 pathogenic variant, splenic artery rupture

lead_in: Which single statement most accurately reflects the most likely diagnosis and the best next step?

options.text: Hypermobile EDS - reassure and focus on physiotherapy and pain management, with no genetic testing arranged
options.rationale: Hypermobile EDS does not explain translucent skin, prominent chest-wall venous pattern, spontaneous pneumothorax, easy bruising, characteristic facial features, or a known familial COL3A1 variant; missing vascular EDS in this context risks a life-threatening vascular event (International EDS classification 2017).

options.text: Vascular EDS - cascade COL3A1 testing, baseline non-invasive vascular imaging, celiprolol if confirmed, plus pregnancy counselling
options.rationale: The combination of translucent skin, characteristic facies, easy bruising in unusual locations, spontaneous pneumothorax, and a first-degree relative with a confirmed pathogenic COL3A1 variant strongly suggests vascular EDS. Cascade targeted testing for her sister's variant is the next step; if confirmed, baseline non-invasive vascular imaging, celiprolol (where available - the BBEST trial showed reduction in arterial events), avoidance of routine arteriography, and detailed pregnancy counselling are recommended (International EDS classification 2017; EDS Society care recommendations).

options.text: Marfan syndrome - arrange FBN1 testing and commence losartan empirically while awaiting confirmation of the diagnosis
options.rationale: There is no marfanoid habitus, no aortic root dilatation, no ectopia lentis, no high-arched palate, and a known familial COL3A1 variant rather than FBN1; Marfan syndrome is not the working diagnosis (Revised Ghent criteria 2010).

options.text: Classical EDS - send COL5A1 and COL5A2 testing rather than the COL3A1 gene that her sister carries
options.rationale: Classical EDS features include atrophic scarring, marked skin hyperextensibility, and widely splayed scars rather than translucent skin, prominent venous pattern, and spontaneous arterial rupture; the family variant is in COL3A1, not COL5 (International EDS classification 2017).

options.text: Confirmed vascular EDS - proceed directly to catheter arteriography to comprehensively map the entire arterial tree
options.rationale: Catheter arteriography in vascular EDS carries a high risk of arterial dissection and is generally avoided; non-invasive cross-sectional vascular imaging (MRA or CTA) is preferred (EDS Society care recommendations).

explanation.summary: Vascular EDS (COL3A1) presents with translucent skin, characteristic facies, easy bruising, spontaneous pneumothorax or hollow viscus rupture, and arterial events that can occur in adolescence. With a known familial pathogenic variant, cascade testing followed by non-invasive vascular imaging, celiprolol (where available), and avoidance of invasive arteriography is the management strategy (International EDS classification 2017).
key_points: Major diagnostic features of vEDS: arterial/intestinal/uterine rupture, family history of vEDS, characteristic facies, easy bruising
Celiprolol reduced arterial events in the BBEST trial; not yet routinely PBS-listed but used internationally
Pregnancy in vEDS carries substantially elevated mortality risk and warrants pre-conception multidisciplinary counselling
explanation.pearls: Avoid arteriography, central venous access where possible, and elective surgery in vEDS - tissue fragility makes surgical complications common and severe.

subtopic_detail: Fragile X - inheritance and carrier counselling
stem: A 32-year-old woman attends a genetics outpatient clinic for counselling after her 5-year-old son was diagnosed with Fragile X syndrome on a clinically indicated FMR1 molecular test showing a full mutation expansion of approximately 380 CGG repeats. Her son has moderate intellectual disability, autistic features, and the classic phenotype. Targeted FMR1 testing of the patient herself reveals a premutation in the 80 CGG repeat range. She is currently considering a second pregnancy and asks specifically about the risk to her future biological children, the implications for her own health, and the implications for her younger brother (aged 28) and her elderly father. Her brother and father are clinically asymptomatic. She has had no fertility issues to date and her menstrual cycles are regular.

data_table.Genetics: Index case (son): FMR1 full mutation (~380 CGG repeats); Mother (patient): FMR1 premutation (~80 CGG repeats)
data_table.Family history: Maternal grandfather alive aged 72, no known clinical issues; brother 28 - asymptomatic

lead_in: Which single statement is the most accurate counselling point?

options.text: Any future daughter who inherits her premutation will definitely develop full-syndrome Fragile X in childhood
options.rationale: Heterozygous females with the full mutation often have variable, milder cognitive phenotype due to X-inactivation; inheriting the premutation, not the full mutation, does not cause full Fragile X syndrome but does carry risk of FXPOI and FXTAS (National Fragile X Foundation).

options.text: Fragile X is autosomal recessive and her brother must be tested before he plans any pregnancy
options.rationale: Fragile X is X-linked, not autosomal recessive; this fundamentally changes carrier risk and reproductive counselling (National Fragile X Foundation).

options.text: Premutation alleles do not expand on transmission and never cause clinical disease in any carrier
options.rationale: Premutation alleles can expand on female-line transmission and cause clinical disease in carriers themselves: fragile X-associated primary ovarian insufficiency (FXPOI) in approximately 20% of female carriers and fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS) in older male carriers (National Fragile X Foundation).

options.text: Her father almost certainly carries the full mutation and has a 50% transmission risk to children
options.rationale: Male premutation carriers do not undergo expansion on paternal transmission - they transmit only a stable premutation to their daughters, and never the full mutation directly; her father almost certainly carries a premutation himself, not a full mutation (National Fragile X Foundation).

options.text: Each future biological son has a 50% inheritance risk, and female-line premutations can expand to full mutation
options.rationale: Fragile X is X-linked. A female premutation carrier transmits the affected X with a 50% probability to each child; only female-line transmission carries the risk of CGG repeat expansion to the full mutation, with the expansion risk rising sharply once maternal premutation length exceeds approximately 90 to 100 repeats. A son who inherits the expanded full mutation will manifest the syndrome (RANZCOG carrier screening; National Fragile X Foundation clinical guidance).

explanation.summary: Fragile X is X-linked with CGG expansion of the FMR1 gene. Premutation (~55-200 repeats) carriers risk female-line expansion to full mutation (>200 repeats) and direct premutation-associated disease (FXPOI in females, FXTAS in older males). Counselling addresses transmission, expansion risk, reproductive options, and broader family cascade testing (National Fragile X Foundation; RANZCOG).
key_points: Premutation: ~55-200 CGG repeats; full mutation: >200 CGG repeats with FMR1 hypermethylation
Expansion to full mutation occurs only with female-line transmission; risk rises with premutation size
Reproductive options: prenatal diagnosis (CVS/amniocentesis), preimplantation genetic testing, gamete donation
explanation.pearls: Female premutation carriers are at risk of fragile X-associated primary ovarian insufficiency - relevant when counselling about timing of family planning.

stem: A 4-year-old previously well girl is brought to her GP because her mother has noticed an increasing number of pale brown patches on her skin over the past 2 years. There is no family history of neurofibromatosis. She is developmentally appropriate, has no learning concerns, is fully immunised, and has no other medical issues. On full examination today she has 7 well-demarcated cafe-au-lait macules across her trunk and limbs, all greater than 5 mm in their longest diameter, and freckling clearly visible in both axillae. She has no Lisch nodules visible on bedside ophthalmoscopy, no plexiform neurofibroma, no cutaneous neurofibroma, no optic glioma symptoms, and no skeletal dysplasia. Her growth is on the 50th centile and her head circumference on the 75th. Vision and developmental screening today are normal.

data_table.Examination: 7 cafe-au-lait macules >5 mm, bilateral axillary freckling, no neurofibromas, no Lisch nodules on bedside exam, no skeletal abnormality

lead_in: Which single statement most accurately reflects whether she meets the NIH diagnostic criteria for neurofibromatosis type 1?

options.text: She does not meet criteria - skin findings alone can never be diagnostic of NF1
options.rationale: The 2021 revised NIH/NF1 diagnostic criteria explicitly allow diagnosis based on multiple cafe-au-lait macules of appropriate size combined with skin-fold freckling, in the absence of a family history (Revised NF1 Diagnostic Criteria 2021).

options.text: She meets criteria only if molecular NF1 gene testing returns a positive pathogenic variant
options.rationale: Molecular confirmation is one diagnostic criterion but not required when two other clinical criteria are present (Revised NF1 diagnostic criteria 2021).

options.text: She does not meet criteria - the cafe-au-lait threshold for diagnosis is at least 10 macules
options.rationale: The diagnostic threshold is 6 or more cafe-au-lait macules >5 mm prepubertally, not 10 (Revised NF1 diagnostic criteria 2021).

options.text: She meets criteria: 6 or more macules >5 mm prepubertally plus skin-fold axillary freckling
options.rationale: The 2021 revised criteria require two or more of: 6 or more cafe-au-lait macules >5 mm prepubertally (>15 mm postpubertally), skin-fold freckling, 2 or more neurofibromas or 1 plexiform NF, optic pathway glioma, 2 or more Lisch nodules or choroidal abnormalities, distinctive osseous lesion, pathogenic NF1 variant, or affected first-degree relative. She has two: macule count/size and freckling (Revised NF1 Diagnostic Criteria 2021).

options.text: She meets criteria only if a first-degree relative also has clinically confirmed NF1
options.rationale: An affected first-degree relative is one of several alternative criteria but is not required when other clinical features are present (Revised NF1 diagnostic criteria 2021).

explanation.summary: Diagnosis of NF1 is clinical, based on the 2021 revised NIH/NF1 criteria requiring two or more defined features. Cafe-au-lait macule threshold is 6 or more, >5 mm prepubertally or >15 mm postpubertally, and skin-fold freckling counts as a separate diagnostic feature (Revised NF1 diagnostic criteria 2021).
key_points: 6 or more cafe-au-lait macules with appropriate size threshold
Skin-fold freckling (axillary or inguinal) is a separate diagnostic feature
Other criteria: neurofibromas, optic glioma, Lisch nodules or choroidal abnormalities, distinctive bone lesion, pathogenic NF1 variant, affected first-degree relative
explanation.pearls: Even with diagnostic certainty by clinical criteria, NF1 children need structured surveillance for optic pathway glioma (under age 7), neurofibroma growth, scoliosis, and learning issues.

stem: A 3-year-old boy with clinically diagnosed neurofibromatosis type 1 (multiple cafe-au-lait macules, bilateral axillary freckling, affected mother) is seen at his general paediatric clinic for a routine review. He is developmentally appropriate, has no headaches, no vomiting, and no obvious visual complaints. He has had no prior dedicated ophthalmology assessment. His mother has been told that NF1 children are at risk of optic pathway glioma but is unclear when surveillance should start and how often it should occur. On examination today he has no obvious visual abnormality on bedside testing, no proptosis, normal pupils, and a fundus that is difficult to assess in clinic. There is no precocious puberty, no growth failure, and no new neurological deficit.

data_table.Examination: Multiple CALMs, bilateral axillary freckling, no proptosis, no precocious puberty, no focal neurology

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate surveillance plan for optic pathway glioma in this child?

options.text: MRI brain and orbits every 6 months as routine surveillance until age 8 regardless of symptoms
options.rationale: Routine screening MRI is not recommended in asymptomatic NF1 children because most optic pathway gliomas are indolent and do not progress to require treatment; MRI is reserved for clinical or ophthalmological concern (RCH CPG: NF1; AAP NF1 health supervision).

options.text: Refer only if visual symptoms develop, with no scheduled ophthalmology or imaging surveillance
options.rationale: Pre-verbal and young children cannot reliably report visual loss; scheduled ophthalmology surveillance detects optic pathway glioma when intervention may still preserve vision (AAP NF1 health supervision).

options.text: Annual paediatric ophthalmology review (acuity, visual fields, fundus) until around age 7 to 8
options.rationale: Symptomatic optic pathway glioma in NF1 almost always presents under age 7. The standard surveillance approach is annual paediatric ophthalmology with formal visual acuity, colour vision, visual fields (when age-appropriate), and dilated fundus examination through early childhood; MRI is reserved for concerning ophthalmology findings (RCH CPG: NF1; AAP NF1 health supervision).

options.text: GP-led bedside visual acuity check at each routine immunisation visit only
options.rationale: Standard primary-care vision checks are not adequately sensitive for optic pathway glioma in NF1; formal paediatric ophthalmology is the recommended modality (RCH CPG: NF1).

options.text: Annual CT brain and orbits with intravenous contrast as routine optic pathway surveillance
options.rationale: CT is not used for optic pathway surveillance in NF1; it delivers ionising radiation that compounds NF1-associated second-tumour risk and is less sensitive than MRI for optic glioma (RCH CPG: NF1).

explanation.summary: Optic pathway glioma is the commonest CNS tumour in childhood NF1 and presents almost exclusively before age 7. Surveillance is by annual paediatric ophthalmology through early childhood, with MRI reserved for clinical or examination concern. Routine screening MRI is not recommended (RCH CPG: NF1).
key_points: Annual paediatric ophthalmology surveillance until approximately age 7 to 8, then less frequent
MRI when clinical concern arises (new strabismus, proptosis, precocious puberty, visual change)
Most NF1 OPGs are indolent and do not need treatment - surveillance triggers intervention only for clinical or imaging progression
explanation.pearls: Precocious puberty in NF1 should trigger MRI of the hypothalamic/optic chiasm region - it can be the first sign of an optic chiasm glioma.

stem: A 7-month-old infant girl is brought to a regional paediatric ED with a 5-day history of new clusters of stereotyped flexion movements occurring on waking and after feeds. The clusters consist of brief symmetrical flexion of the neck, trunk, and arms occurring every 5 to 10 seconds for up to 2 minutes, after which she appears distressed and then drowsy. Her mother has captured a video showing typical infantile spasms. She was born at term following an uncomplicated pregnancy, but a foetal MRI at 30 weeks performed because of a cardiac rhabdomyoma seen on antenatal scan showed multiple cortical tubers. A postnatal genetic panel confirmed a pathogenic TSC2 variant. On general examination today she has 3 hypopigmented ash-leaf macules on her trunk and a developmental regression noted by her parents - she had been babbling and reaching for objects 6 weeks ago but is now less responsive and no longer reaches. Her cardiac rhabdomyoma is being followed by cardiology and is currently asymptomatic. EEG performed today shows hypsarrhythmia.

data_table.Examination: 3 ash-leaf macules, regression of social and motor milestones, no focal neurology
data_table.Imaging: Multiple cortical tubers on prior MRI; cardiac rhabdomyoma stable

lead_in: Which single first-line therapy is most appropriate?

options.text: Levetiracetam monotherapy
options.rationale: Levetiracetam is not first-line for infantile spasms; randomised evidence supports vigabatrin (in TSC) and hormonal therapy (otherwise) as first-line (RCH CPG: Infantile spasms).

options.text: Oral prednisolone monotherapy
options.rationale: Hormonal therapy (prednisolone or ACTH) is first-line for infantile spasms without an identified cause, but in TSC-related spasms vigabatrin is preferred because randomised data show superior efficacy in this subgroup (International TSC Consensus 2021).

options.text: Sodium valproate monotherapy
options.rationale: Sodium valproate is not first-line for infantile spasms and is generally avoided in young female infants because of the long-term reproductive teratogenicity profile and lower efficacy than hormonal therapy or vigabatrin (RCH CPG: Infantile spasms).

options.text: Vigabatrin monotherapy
options.rationale: In infantile spasms occurring in the context of tuberous sclerosis complex, vigabatrin is the first-line agent and is preferred over ACTH or prednisolone because of superior efficacy in this specific subgroup; visual field monitoring is required because of the recognised risk of peripheral visual field loss (RCH CPG: Infantile spasms; International TSC Consensus Recommendations 2021).

options.text: Ketogenic diet (first-line)
options.rationale: The ketogenic diet is a recognised option for refractory infantile spasms but is not first-line; pharmacological control with vigabatrin should be attempted first in TSC (International TSC Consensus 2021).

explanation.summary: Infantile spasms with hypsarrhythmia in TSC are treated first with vigabatrin, which has superior efficacy in this subgroup compared with hormonal therapy. Visual field monitoring is essential because of the recognised risk of peripheral visual field constriction (International TSC Consensus 2021).
key_points: Vigabatrin first-line for TSC-related infantile spasms
Hormonal therapy (prednisolone or ACTH) first-line in non-TSC infantile spasms
Early recognition and treatment improves developmental outcome
explanation.pearls: An antenatal cardiac rhabdomyoma is a strong predictor of tuberous sclerosis - early genetic confirmation enables proactive surveillance for spasms, autism, and TAND.

stem: A 34-year-old G2P1 woman is referred to a tertiary foetal medicine service after a 28-week morphology surveillance ultrasound demonstrated a 14 mm well-circumscribed echogenic mass on the interventricular septum of the foetus, with a second 8 mm mass on the left ventricular free wall. The foetal cardiac function appears preserved on echocardiography, there is no hydrops, no significant outflow tract obstruction, and amniotic fluid volume is normal. The woman has no personal history of seizures, no facial angiofibromas, no hypopigmented macules, and a normal renal ultrasound performed last year. There is no family history of tuberous sclerosis, intellectual disability, or unexplained kidney tumours. She has no other foetal anomalies on dedicated reassessment of the brain, kidneys, and skeleton (acknowledging the limits of antenatal imaging). The foetal medicine team is counselling her about implications.

data_table.Imaging: Foetal echo: 14 mm IVS rhabdomyoma + 8 mm LV free wall rhabdomyoma, preserved function
data_table.Family history: No known TSC; maternal clinical examination unremarkable

lead_in: Which single statement is the most accurate counselling point?

options.text: Multiple foetal rhabdomyomas are strongly associated with TSC; arrange foetal MRI and postnatal TSC1/TSC2 testing
options.rationale: Multiple foetal cardiac rhabdomyomas are highly suggestive of TSC, with around 75 to 80% of foetuses with multiple rhabdomyomas ultimately confirmed to have TSC. Foetal MRI looks for cortical tubers and subependymal nodules, and postnatal molecular testing (TSC1/TSC2) confirms the diagnosis (International TSC Consensus 2021; RCH CPG: Tuberous sclerosis).

options.text: Cardiac rhabdomyomas are almost always isolated benign tumours unrelated to any syndromic genetic diagnosis
options.rationale: Although the lesions themselves are benign and frequently regress, multiple foetal rhabdomyomas are strongly associated with TSC; calling them isolated misses the syndromic diagnosis (International TSC Consensus 2021).

options.text: Antenatal cardiac rhabdomyoma is essentially diagnostic of Williams syndrome and warrants 7q11.23 microarray testing
options.rationale: Williams syndrome features supravalvular aortic stenosis, elfin facies, and hypercalcaemia - not cardiac rhabdomyomas (AAP Williams syndrome health supervision).

options.text: Antenatal cardiac rhabdomyoma is most commonly associated with Noonan syndrome and the RASopathy genes
options.rationale: Noonan syndrome cardiac involvement is dominated by valvular pulmonary stenosis and hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, not rhabdomyomas (Noonan syndrome clinical management guidelines).

options.text: The lesions typically grow rapidly after birth and surgical excision is required in the neonatal period
options.rationale: Cardiac rhabdomyomas typically peak in the third trimester and regress spontaneously after birth; surgical intervention is reserved for haemodynamic obstruction or refractory arrhythmia (International TSC Consensus 2021).

explanation.summary: Multiple antenatal cardiac rhabdomyomas should trigger evaluation for tuberous sclerosis complex with foetal brain MRI and postnatal molecular testing of TSC1/TSC2. The cardiac lesions themselves usually regress, but the syndromic implications drive longitudinal surveillance for spasms, TAND, renal and pulmonary disease (International TSC Consensus 2021).
key_points: Multiple foetal rhabdomyomas: about 75-80% are associated with TSC
Single rhabdomyoma: TSC association lower but still warrants assessment
Cardiac lesions typically regress; surveillance is for syndromic, not cardiac, indications
explanation.pearls: Postnatal mTOR inhibitor therapy (sirolimus or everolimus) can shrink TSC-related tumours including SEGAs, renal angiomyolipomas, and rhabdomyomas in selected cases.

subtopic_detail: 22q11.2 deletion syndrome - hypocalcaemia and immune workup
stem: A 5-day-old term male neonate is reviewed on the postnatal ward after a generalised tonic-clonic seizure lasting 90 seconds, which terminated spontaneously. He was born at 38+5 weeks gestation by uncomplicated normal vaginal delivery; antenatal scans had identified a conotruncal cardiac anomaly (interrupted aortic arch type B) for which he is scheduled for surgical repair next week at a tertiary cardiothoracic service. On examination he has subtle dysmorphic features including hypertelorism, a broad nasal bridge, low-set ears with deficient ear lobes, and a small mouth with a high-arched palate. He has not had a thymic shadow on his postnatal chest radiograph, although the team noted that thymic absence is difficult to confirm radiographically. Capillary samples now show a markedly low ionised calcium. Magnesium and glucose are within normal range and there has been no exposure to maternal calcium-altering medications. The neonatal team is planning a coordinated investigation pathway with cardiology and immunology.

data_table.Examination: Hypertelorism, broad nasal bridge, low-set ears, high-arched palate; cardiac murmur of IAA-B
data_table.Investigations: Corrected calcium 1.62 mmol/L, ionised calcium 0.78 mmol/L, magnesium 0.85 mmol/L, glucose 3.4 mmol/L, PTH inappropriately low
data_table.Imaging: No clear thymic shadow on CXR; IAA-B confirmed on echo

lead_in: Which single investigation is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Chromosomal microarray to detect the 22q11.2 microdeletion
options.rationale: The combination of conotruncal cardiac anomaly (interrupted aortic arch type B), hypocalcaemia with inappropriately low PTH, suspected thymic hypoplasia, and characteristic facial features is the classic DiGeorge/velocardiofacial presentation. Chromosomal microarray is the first-line test to detect the 22q11.2 microdeletion (more sensitive than FISH for atypical deletion sizes) (RCH CPG: 22q11.2 deletion syndrome; International 22q11.2 Deletion Syndrome Foundation guidelines).

options.text: Standard karyotype only, looking specifically for trisomy 22
options.rationale: 22q11.2 deletion is a sub-microscopic microdeletion not visible on standard karyotype; microarray (or targeted FISH) is required (RCH CPG: 22q11.2 deletion syndrome).

options.text: Repeat newborn screen card for congenital hypothyroidism
options.rationale: Repeat newborn screen for thyroid function does not address conotruncal anomaly, hypocalcaemia and thymic hypoplasia and is not the appropriate next test in this syndromic context (RCH CPG: Neonatal hypocalcaemia).

options.text: Maternal vitamin D level only at this stage
options.rationale: Maternal vitamin D deficiency causes neonatal hypocalcaemia but does not account for the cardiac anomaly, thymic absence, and facial features - microarray is needed to confirm the unifying diagnosis (RCH CPG: Neonatal hypocalcaemia).

options.text: Trans-fontanelle cranial ultrasound at the cotside
options.rationale: Cranial ultrasound does not investigate the unifying genetic syndrome and is not indicated in an afebrile neonate with hypocalcaemic seizure plus syndromic features; microarray is the next test (RCH CPG: 22q11.2 deletion syndrome).

explanation.summary: The triad of conotruncal cardiac anomaly, hypoparathyroid hypocalcaemia, and thymic hypoplasia (CATCH-22 features) in a dysmorphic neonate points to 22q11.2 deletion syndrome (DiGeorge / velocardiofacial). Chromosomal microarray is the first-line genetic test; immunology (T-cell subsets) should run in parallel, and live vaccines are deferred until immune competence is confirmed (RCH CPG: 22q11.2 deletion syndrome).
key_points: Cardiac anomalies: interrupted aortic arch type B, truncus arteriosus, tetralogy of Fallot are typical
Defer live attenuated vaccines (e.g. rotavirus, BCG, MMR, varicella) until immune competence confirmed
Calcium, T-cell subset and immunoglobulin assessment guide ongoing care
explanation.pearls: Velopharyngeal insufficiency, hypernasal speech, and learning difficulties often dominate the phenotype in older children with 22q11.2 deletion.
